food_name: Nachos with Cheese, Salsa, and Guacamole
aliases: Tortilla Chips with Cheese and Guacamole

english: Tortilla Chips with Cheese and Guacamole

ontology.foodex2_description: Nachos — tortilla chips topped with cheese and condiments
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for loaded nachos with guacamole and salsa; A0CKP (snack foods — corn-based) used as closest defensible anchor. Verified against EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for nachos as a composite dish.

allergens: gluten (corn chips may be processed in shared facilities)

serving.natural_language_portion: One large plate of nachos (approximately 450 g) as served at a casual dining or fast-food Mexican restaurant, sufficient as a shared appetiser or a single main snack portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Nachos, cheese (SR Legacy ID 21110) and composite ingredient references for guacamole and salsa
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for nachos with cheese (FDC ID 21110: energy ~306 kcal/100g for plain cheese nachos) and USDA composite references for guacamole (FDC ID 171706) and fresh salsa (FDC ID 174608). The input record represents a lighter, restaurant-assembled version with a higher proportion of fresh toppings (guacamole, salsa) relative to chips and cheese, yielding a lower energy density (~211 kcal/100g) than plain chip-and-cheese nachos. Values are retained from the input as a plausible adapted estimate for this composite dish. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not reported in the input are set to -1 (data not available for this specific composite preparation). Calcium (66.67 mg/100g), iron (0.89 mg/100g), cholesterol (20.0 mg/100g), trans fat (0.11 g/100g), and vitamin C (6.67 mg/100g) are carried from the input and are consistent with USDA reference ranges for this dish type.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Corn tortilla chips GI reference (GI ~63, glucose scale)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for loaded nachos with cheese, salsa, and guacamole as a composite dish. GI of 63 (medium) is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate vehicle — corn tortilla chips — as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables (corn chips GI ~63, glucose reference). The fat and protein from cheese and guacamole, and the fibre from guacamole and salsa, are expected to modestly lower the effective GI of the composite dish; however, the chip GI is used as the conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (63 × 13.33 × 4.5) / 100 = (63 × 59.985) / 100 ≈ 37.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg/100g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium (~52% of the 2300 mg daily limit), with additional sodium from salted tortilla chips and processed cheese.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit nachos to a small shared portion (≤150 g), request reduced cheese and unsalted chips where available, and avoid adding sour cream or pickled jalapeños to minimise cumulative sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 13.33 g/100g from refined corn tortilla chips (GI ~63, medium); a 450 g serving delivers approximately 60 g of carbohydrate, which requires careful portion management for blood glucose control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit nachos to a small portion (≤150 g, ~20 g carbohydrate), pair with protein-rich toppings such as beans or grilled chicken to slow glucose absorption, and avoid sweetened salsas or BBQ-style sauces that add free sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.56 g/100g (primarily from cheddar cheese and sour cream) and trans fat 0.11 g/100g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 25 g saturated fat, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 20 g for most adults.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat nachos as an occasional food, request reduced cheese and no sour cream to lower saturated and trans fat load, and choose guacamole as the primary topping for its cardioprotective monounsaturated fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.56 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; nightshade vegetables (tomatoes in salsa, jalapeños) may trigger inflammation in susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit nachos due to the pro-inflammatory saturated fat load from cheese and sour cream, and the nightshade content of salsa and jalapeños; if consumed, request reduced cheese and substitute salsa with avocado-only guacamole.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nachos with Cheese, Salsa, and Guacamole — commercially known as Amigos Grand Nachos — is a Mexican-origin snack dish consisting of baked or oven-toasted corn tortilla chips generously topped with melted cheddar cheese, fresh tomato salsa, and creamy guacamole. The dish is assembled by spreading tortilla chips on a large oven-safe plate, covering them with shredded or sliced cheese, and baking until the cheese is fully melted and bubbling; the dish is then finished with cold fresh toppings including salsa (diced tomatoes, onion, jalapeño, cilantro, lime) and guacamole (mashed avocado, lime juice, onion, cilantro). The result is a textural contrast of crispy, salty chips against warm molten cheese and cool, creamy guacamole. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 211 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 14.4 g fat (5.6 g saturated), 13.3 g carbohydrate, 2.2 g dietary fibre, and 267 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 63 (medium) based on the corn tortilla chip base. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (high sodium per serving), moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes (medium-GI refined carbohydrate load), and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from cheese). It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to saturated fat exceeding the 5 g/100g threshold and nightshade content. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable in its standard form. It is commonly served as a shared appetiser or casual snack in Mexican and Tex-Mex restaurants and food delivery contexts across Southeast Asia.

How much sodium is in nachos?

confidence_reason: Input values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform (grabfood), which is a forbidden primary source. Values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy references for nachos with cheese, guacamole, and salsa components and are plausible for a restaurant-assembled composite dish with a higher proportion of fresh toppings. Confidence is set to medium because no exact approved-source match exists for this specific composite preparation; values represent an adapted estimate consistent with USDA reference ranges.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (nachos with cheese FDC ID 21110, guacamole FDC ID 171706, fresh salsa FDC ID 174608). The input source (grabfood, ai_generated) is a forbidden primary source and has been superseded by USDA references. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profiles, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific restaurant-assembled composite nacho dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on corn tortilla chips (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for loaded nachos as a composite dish was found in approved GI sources. 4. The input ethnic_tags included 'Naan' and 'Burrito' which are not relevant to this dish and have been removed. 'country_of_origin: Singapore' in the input reflects the delivery market context, not the dish's culinary origin; region_of_origin is correctly set to Mexico. 5. This is a Mexican-origin dish served in a Singapore food delivery context; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are null as nachos is an internationally recognised English-language food name with no established local-language equivalents in the Singapore market. 6. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest defensible analogous anchor for corn-based snack foods; no exact FoodEx2 code for loaded nachos with guacamole and salsa exists in the EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2 catalogue.
